author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
title: God Commands No Impossibilities.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: What he says, however, is true enough, “that God is as good as just, and made man such that he was quite able to live without the evil of sin, if only he had been willing.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For who does not know that man was made whole and faultless, and endowed with a free will and a free ability to lead a holy life?

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Our present inquiry, however, is about the man whom “the thieves” left half dead on the road, and who, being disabled and pierced through with heavy wounds, is not so able to mount up to the heights of righteousness as he was able to descend therefrom; who, moreover, if he is now in “the inn,” is in process of cure.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: God therefore does not command impossibilities; but in His command He counsels you both to do what you can for yourself, and to ask His aid in what you cannot do.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Now, we should see whence comes the possibility, and whence the impossibility.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: This man says: “That proceeds not from a man’s will which he can do by nature.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: I say: A man is not righteous by his will if he can be by nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: He will, however, be able to accomplish by remedial aid what he is rendered incapable of doing by his flaw.
